spec_match.rationale: Two tagged-template helpers (`re` and `likePattern`) feed every interpolation / literal byte through `RegExp.escape`, which is the canonical user-input-safe pattern the proposal motivates.

interactive.rationale: Two side-by-side editable panels — a regex builder with an interpolated term + haystack, and a SQL-LIKE pattern with log lines — both rendering live highlights on every keystroke.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature index promises `re escapes interpolations, likePattern turns SQL-LIKE wildcards into regex while escaping every literal byte` — both panels deliver exactly that.

edge_cases.rationale: Covers happy-path use of both tags but never shows what would have happened without the escape (no naive vs safe panel), and never tests an interpolation containing a backslash, newline, or surrogate pair which the spec hex-escapes.

references.rationale: The `see also` list only links sibling concept pages. Missing the chromestatus.com/feature/5179154657705984 link required by CLAUDE.md invariant #3 (and the TC39 proposal that defines what the tag is meant to do).

design_system.rationale: Inline styles use CSS variables only, no raw hex, no button overrides — clean.

openQuestions.title: Add the missing chromestatus reference link
openQuestions.detail: Per CLAUDE.md invariant #3, every concept page must cite `https://chromestatus.com/feature/5179154657705984` so the slug-recovery cleanup script can self-heal. This page currently links only siblings. Add the link to `see also`.

openQuestions.title: Show what `re` does without escape (naive-vs-safe)
openQuestions.detail: All other regexp-escape concepts contrast naive `new RegExp(input)` against the safe path. This page just shows the safe one. Add a small `without re tag (raw)` rendering next to each panel so the value of the tag is visible — try `$5.00 (USD)` raw and watch the `(USD)` become a capture group.

openQuestions.title: Cover the spec's hex-escape edge case in `likePattern`
openQuestions.detail: Add an example where the literal text includes a newline, tab, or surrogate pair — the spec mandates `\xHH`/`\uHHHH` escapes for those, but a one-char `ch === '%' ? '.*' : RegExp.escape(ch)` loop will quietly emit the correct output without flagging it. A debug strip showing the per-char escape output would surface this.

openQuestions.title: Make the LIKE example actually use a wildcard
openQuestions.detail: The default `error.%(timeout|reset)` looks like it should be a wildcard but the `|` and `()` are literal in SQL-LIKE — the demo's own escaping converts them to literals. Replace the default with a clearer example like `error.%timeout%` or `error._ at%` to demonstrate the `%` and `_` wildcards distinctly.

summary: Nice authoring-tag demo with two genuinely different real-world use cases. Two gaps: missing the chromestatus self-heal link, and the default LIKE pattern uses regex-style alternation that the LIKE tag promptly escapes — confusing for the reader. A short naive-vs-safe contrast on the `re` panel would lift it from `nice tag library` to `here is what the tag is buying you`.
